summary: Census interrogation form — a structured document that asks 'who?' (pi/piqa) eighteen times across administrative dimensions. 253 cords, 20 STRING (100% hits). 9 consecutive pi (who?) in a 3x3 COLOR GRID (MB x3, KB x3, W x3 = interrogation in 3 dimensions), then 9 piqa (who-TOPIC) as answer registers (DB x3 + BB x6). Only 2 non-interrogative words: chaqa (bridge) + qaqa (rock) = geographic markers. Published by Ascher & Ascher 1987 pp. 62-65. Museum of Science, Buffalo, NY. Unique in the corpus.
interpretation:
IDENTIFICATION
  253 cords. 20 STRING (8%, 100% hits), 142 INT, 91 EMPTY (36%).
  Architecture: 1-PAIRE, depth L3. 43 clusters.
  Colors: MB(111), KB(56), W(20), YB(17), DB(16), BB(~15) — 7+ colors.
  Provenance: Unknown. Museum: Museum of Science, Buffalo, New York (C14459).
  Published: Ascher & Ascher 1987, pp. 62-65.
  OKR: Cord 2 'attachment loose, movable'. Cord 72 'formed into a loop'.

VOCABULARY
  pi (who?) x9 — consecutive cords 91-99, 3x3 color grid: MB(3), KB(3), W(3)
  piqa (who-TOPIC) x9 — cords 216-219 (DB x3), 235-240 (BB x6)
  chaqa (bridge/slaughter) x1 — cord 58, MB
  qaqa (rock) x1 — cord 59, MB
  90% of STRING = interrogatives. Unprecedented.

THE 3x3 INTERROGATIVE GRID (cords 91-99)
  MB: pi, pi, pi = 'who? [in this category]'
  KB: pi, pi, pi = 'who? [with these goods]'
  W:  pi, pi, pi = 'who? [in this data]'
  3 colors x 3 repetitions = systematic questioning across 3 administrative dimensions.

THE ANSWER REGISTER (cords 216-240)
  DB: piqa x3 (cords 216-219) = 'concerning whom' — first group of identified persons
  BB: piqa x6 (cords 235-240) = 'concerning whom' — second group
  9 persons identified in response to 9 questions.

STRUCTURE
  Cords 1-57: Pre-interrogation data (numbers + empties)
  Cords 58-59: chaqa + qaqa = geographic markers
  Cords 91-99: THE INTERROGATION (pi x9, 3x3 grid)
  Cords 100-215: Post-interrogation data (answers)
  Cords 216-219: Answer register 1 (piqa x3, DB)
  Cords 235-240: Answer register 2 (piqa x6, BB)

NARRATIVE — THE EIGHTEEN QUESTIONS
  Who? That is the only question this khipu asks — and it asks it eighteen times.
  The document begins with data. One hundred and forty-two numbers, interspersed with ninety-one empty cords — a vast, sparse register of quantities waiting to be assigned to names. At cords 58-59, two geographic markers appear: *chaqa* and *qaqa*. The terrain is established.
  Then, at cord 91, the questioning begins. Nine consecutive cords, each carrying the same word: *pi* — who? But the scribe does not merely repeat the question nine times. He asks it in three colors. The first three *pi* are on medium-brown — the color of categories. Who belongs to this category? The next three are on dark-brown — the color of objects. Who holds these goods? The last three are on white — the color of data. Who produced these quantities? A three-by-three grid of interrogatives, each color representing one dimension of the census.
  The answers are not immediate. More than a hundred cords of numerical data follow — the counts, the measurements, the tribute amounts that will be attached to names once the questions are answered.
  Then, at cord 216, the answers begin. *Piqa* — who, as a topic. 'Concerning this person.' Three entries on dark-brown, then six more on another color. Nine persons identified, nine answers to the eighteen questions.
  The Aschers published this khipu in 1987. They noted the irregular attachments, the loops, the movable pendants. They did not have a syllabary to read the knots as words. What they saw as structural anomalies are the FORM. A census questionnaire, knotted in cotton thread, asking the same question in three administrative dimensions.
references: Ascher, M. & Ascher, R. (1987). Mathematics of the Incas: Code of the Quipu, pp. 62-65
Sivan 2026, ALBA Project — Census interrogation form, 3x3 grid discovery
Museum of Science, Buffalo, New York (C14459)
Cross-corpus: UR193 (Pachacamac oracle, concentrated interrogatives), UR037 (census, kiki x11)
